articles.title: How Ranveer Allahbadia’s Controversy on ‘India’s Got Latent’ could bring in more stringent OTT regulations
articles.summary: In the Ranveer Allahbadia case, the Supreme Court has signaled its disapproval of the existing digital content and creator ecology. We look closely at the court proceedings that potentially paving the way for the revival of the shelved Broadcasting Bill.
articles.content: Donate Now tl;dr In the Ranveer Allahbadia case, the Supreme Court has signaled its disapproval of the existing digital content and creator ecology. We look closely at the court proceedings that potentially paving the way for the revival of the shelved Broadcasting Bill. Background In early February 2025, video clips from comedian Samay Raina’s YouTube panel show India’s Got Latent — featuring Ranveer “BeerBiceps” Allahbadia, Ashish Chanchlani, Jaspreet Singh, and Apoorva Mukhija—went viral. Some viewers condemned the jokes, which used explicit sexual references to parents, as vulgar and obscene. The backlash centred on Raina and Allahbadia. On 10 February 2025 the Guwahati Police registered a first‑information report (“FIR”) against the five creators for “promoting obscenity”, citing: (a) Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (“BNS”) Sections 79, 95, 294, 296; (b) Cinematograph Act, 1952 Sections 4, 7; and the (c) Indecent Representation of Women (Prohibition) Act, 1986 Sections 4, 6. The Maharashtra Cyber Department followed suit, booking them under BNS Sections 79, 196, 296, 299 and s. 67 of the Information Technology Act, 2000 at Nodal Cyber Police Station, Thane. Press reports also point to an FIR in Jaipur . Confronted with parallel proceedings in three states, Allahbadia petitioned the Supreme Court (W.P. (Crl.) 85/2025) to quash—or at least consolidate—the Assam and Maharashtra cases. His lawyers mentioned the case before a Bench headed by the Chief Justice of India on 14 February for urgent listing, and the Court scheduled it for hearing on 18 February 2025. Timeline of court proceedings The legal roller‑coaster began on 14 February 2025, when Ranveer Allahbadia hurried to the Supreme Court seeking protection from arrest and a cascade of FIRs registered across the country over an episode of India’s Got Latent . On 18 February 2025, the Bench granted him interim relief but attached four tight conditions: he must cooperate fully with investigators, surrender his passport, remain in India, and halt all new episodes of his show. During the hearing Justice Vikram Nath stated the programme’s language “dirty” and “perverted.” Excerpt from the SC order dated 18.02.2025 in W.P. (Crl.) 83 of 2025 That same afternoon Ashish Chanchlani, another panellist, secured parallel interim protection from the Gauhati High Court; three days later, the Supreme Court formally tagged his case to Allahbadia’s petition. Excerpt from the Guwahati HC order dated 18.02.2025 in Case No. : AB/359/2025 A semblance of reprieve arrived on 3 March. Acknowledging that the podcast supported roughly 280 jobs, the Supreme Court allowed Allahbadia to restart production—provided he kept the conversation “decent” and steered clear of matters under judicial consideration. Crucially, the Bench asked the Solicitor General to draft proposals for a national regime to regulate online content without trampling free‑speech safeguards. This gave rise to fears of the revival of the Broadcasting Bill which IFF has commented on and analysed extensively. Excerpt from the SC order dated 03.03.2025 in W.P. (Crl.) 83 of 2025 Procedural hearings followed. On 7 March the Gauhati High Court extended anticipatory bail to Chanchlani, while Allahbadia, on 1 April, filed an affidavit detailing his cooperation with the National Commission for Women and cyber‑police officials. The Solicitor General reported that investigations in Maharashtra and Assam were nearing completion, so the Court kept interim protections alive. The case soon intersected with disability rights. Excerpt from the Guwahati HC order dated 07.03.2025 in Case No. : AB/359/2025 Excerpt from the SC order dated 01.04.2025 in W.P. (Crl.) 83 of 2025 On 21 April, the State of Maharashtra informed the Court that its investigation was complete and a charge sheet would be filed soon; Assam stated that its investigation was almost complete. Excerpt from the SC order dated 21.04.2025 in W.P. (Crl.) 83 of 2025 Meanwhile, M/s Cure SMA Foundation sought to join the matter, objecting to Samay Raina’s allegedly ableist jokes. The Court agreed to consider the intervention and asked for a full petition, stressing that creative liberty cannot override the dignity of marginalised communities. Subsequently, matters escalated on the hearing on 28 April. While hearing Cure SMA Foundation’s petition, court observed that the issue of content regulation on OTT platforms falls within the domain of policy-making. The petition had raised concerns regarding the alleged circulation of vulgar and inappropriate material on streaming platforms. While the Court declined to frame regulations itself, citing the principle of separation of powers, it directed the petitioner to serve a copy of the petition to the Central Government for its consideration. On the same date of hearing, the Supreme Court allowed Allahbadia to retrieve his passport but refused to club the Maharashtra and Assam FIRs on that date. Justice Surya Kant reasoned that the Assam complaint alleged distinct offences and its residents should not be forced to litigate in Mumbai. The Bench also chastised police for publicly insinuating Allahbadia’s guilt before filing a chargesheet. That day it instructed Cure SMA to obtain details from Mumbai’s Cyber Cell so every relevant respondents could served and be brought on record. Excerpt from the SC order dated 28.04.2025 in W.P. (Crl.) 83 of 2025 Finally, on 5 May, the Supreme Court ordered the Mumbai Police Commissioner to serve notices on Samay Raina and four other creators, warning that coercive measures would follow any no‑show. The judges reiterated their intention to frame comprehensive guidelines for online content—a signal that the spirit of the shelved Broadcasting Bill might soon re‑emerge in regulatory form. These proceedings have given a rise to concern and alarm. Excerpt from the SC order dated 05.05.2025 in W.P. (Crl.) 83 of 2025 The judges reiterated their intention to frame comprehensive guidelines for online content—a signal that the spirit of the shelved Broadcasting Bill might soon re‑emerge in regulatory form. These proceedings have given a rise to concern and alarm. Excerpt from Live Law reporting dated 05.05.2025 in W.P. (Crl.) 83 of 2025 Proactive Move by MIB: Advisory to OTT Platforms on Content Regulation On 20 February 2025, two days after the Supreme Court lamented a “vacuum in law” on digital speech, the Ministry of Information and Broadcasting (“MIB”) issued an advisory telling over‑the‑top (OTT) services to obey existing rules. Citing a spike in complaints from MPs, regulators and parents about “obscene, pornographic and vulgar” videos, the ministry reminded platforms that the IT Rules 2021 Code of Ethics, the Indecent Representation of Women Act, the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita 2023, the POCSO Act and the Information Technology Act 2000 all criminalise unlawful sexual content. It urged stricter age ratings and warned the two self‑regulatory bodies for OTTs to act against violations. While some may state that the advisory adds no new obligations; it merely re‑states laws that already bind streamers. Even so, the timing—just a week after a parliamentary panel decried “vulgar content” online—suggests the government is preparing the ground for tougher, possibly pre‑screening, controls on digital media. Excerpt from the advisory dated 19.02.2025 issued by MIB While the term "regulation" may sound like an innocuous word, it’s important to recall that the State’s previously proposed legal framework - Broadcasting Services (Regulation) Bill, 2023 (“Broadcasting Bill”), which was so draconian that it faced widespread criticism and was ultimately shelved. The MIB released the Broadcasting Bill 2023 for public consultation on November 10, 2023. The draft bill included “Over-the-Top” (“OTT”) content & digital news published by individuals under the regulatory ambit raising concerns for online free speech and journalistic freedom. Subsequently, in July 2024, another version of the Broadcasting Bill was released but never officially published by MIB, however, several unofficial copies of the 2024 Bill were circulated online. The 2024 Bill reportedly imposed compliance obligations on digital creators impacting their online free speech and creative expression, increased the ambit of persons/ entities regulated under the Bill, retained and introduced ambiguous definitions for significant provisions, and negatively impacted users of online platforms. Read more about the Bill here and here . Last year, amid severe pushback and criticism, the MIB reportedly asked stakeholders to return physical copies of the 2024 Broadcasting Bill, promising to reconsider certain provisions. On August 12, 2024, in a post on X, MIB announced that a revised draft would follow consultations, with the comment deadline extended to October 15. Since then, there has been no update. Despite the MIB’s continued opacity, cosmetic changes won’t fix the Bill’s deep flaws. We demand that the MIB #KillTheBill to protect free speech for digital creators, political commentators, users and other key stakeholders. We have been monitoring these developments and will continue to do so closely. Important Documents IFF’s First Read of the Broadcasting Services (Regulation) Bill, 2023 ( Link ) IFF’s summary of the developments around the Broadcasting Bill since July 2024 ( Link ) SC’s March 3, 2025 order directing the Solicitor General to draft proposals on online content regulation ( Link )
